Nakaseke — Nakaseke district Chairman Ignatius Koomu has said every homestead in the district will be compelled to have a permanent plantation of food crops in an effort to fight famine and poverty in the district.
Koomu said the policy that was passed by the district executive starts early next year. "We have passed a resolution that each homestead in the district must have a plantation of food crops before engaging in any other business," Koomu said.

Original_Search_Title: Newmont Ghana Gold Assists MOFA With Motorbikes
Headline: Ghana: Newmont Ghana Gold Assists MOFA With Motorbikes
Body_Text:
Newmont Ghana Gold Limited, as part of its social responsibilities to sustain development in livelihood and improve agriculture, has presented ten Yamaha motorbikes to the Asutifi District Office of the Ministry of Food Agriculture (MOFA).
The motorbikes, which cost an amount of $3,500 each, were presented to 10 extension officers who are in connection with Newmont's Agricultural Improvement and Land Access Program (AILAP) at Kenyasi.

Original_Search_Title: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Headline: Africa: Kaunda is a Man to Admire, But Africa Doesn't Need Such Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A photograph on the cover of the October 19 issue of the Daily Monitor, Uganda's main independent newspaper, shows President Yoweri Museveni shaking hands with Zambia's former president Kenneth Kaunda (clutching his signature white handkerchief).
Looking on is Rwanda's President Paul Kagame. The picture was taken at the Sandton Convention Centre, Johannesburg, during the Annual Business Leaders' Forum. What is striking about the photo is that, with their heads and chins clean-shaven, Museveni and Kaunda look to be about the same age. However, Museveni is 62 years old, and Kaunda is all of 82!

Original_Search_Title: Is Mining Everything?
Headline: Ghana: Is Mining Everything?
Body_Text:
I DO not know if you read the image-building write-up by the Media Relations Unit of Ghana Chamber of Commerce as published in the Wednesday, October 25, 2006, issue of the Daily Graphic.
The statements and statistics were truly impressive, as the write-up presented a picture of a caring Chamber not only concerned about corporate profits but also about the communities in which the mines operate.

Original_Search_Title: Biofuel Begins to Attract As Crude Oil Prices Fluctuate
Headline: Ghana: Biofuel Begins to Attract As Crude Oil Prices Fluctuate
Body_Text:
Accra — When Ghana's Vice President Alhaji Aliu Mahama visited Thailand at the beginning of September this year, he was fascinated by that country's level of technological advancement in biofuel generation.
According to media reports including that of the Ghana News Agency, the Deputy Director of the Royal "Chitralada" Projects, Ms Rosarin Smitabhim told the Vice President that Thailand would be switching to the use of bio-diesel and gasoline next year.

Original_Search_Title: OVOP to Open Shops
Headline: Malawi: OVOP to Open Shops
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — The One Village One Product (OVOP) secretariat will soon establish shops in Blantyre and Lilongwe where all OVOP products will be stocked and sold to members of the public, The Chronicle has learnt.
The new OVOP National Coordinator, Kamia Kaluma Sulumba told The Chronicle in an interview that the shops will be opened so that farmers have easy access to a market for their products.

Original_Search_Title: Scrapping School Tax Not Enough
Headline: Uganda: Scrapping School Tax Not Enough
Body_Text:
Kampala — Last week during a function at St. Lawrence School, President Yoweri Museveni said the Government was working on scrapping income tax on educational institutions.
Currently, schools are paying a 30% tax on their profits like bars or discotheques, which provide no far reaching strategic benefit to the country.

Original_Search_Title: Bunyoro a Potential National Food Basket
Headline: Uganda: Bunyoro a Potential National Food Basket
Body_Text:
Kampala — Morris Baguma whistles as he harvests comb after comb of maize from his shamba. His 20-acre shamba is located in Kinyabutunzi village, Bugahya county in Hoima district. His wife and farm helper follow him, clearing away whatever comb Baguma might have failed to pick. This is one of the most fertile areas of Bunyoro. "I am going to dry the maize, then sell it later to the buyers from the city," he says.
Bunyoro is one of the areas in Uganda that are gifted with fertile soils. Where as Ankole can only grow bananas and keep cattle, Bunyoro can grow cereals including maize, sorghum, groundnuts, millet and rice, and keep all kinds of animals. Potatoes, Cassava and other food crops can all sustainably be grown in Bunyoro. It can also grow tobacco and sugar canes. There is also fish from Lake Albert. Cereals are mainly grown in the fertile areas of Bugahya in Hoima, Buyanja, Buliisa and areas that formerly formed part of Budongo forest in Masindi.

Original_Search_Title: 'Nigeria Has Waxed Stronger Under Obasanjo'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Nigeria Has Waxed Stronger Under Obasanjo'
Body_Text:
Yola — As the governorship race in Adamawa State enters the crucial and last lap, one of the front runners for Dougerie lodge, the seat of power in the state, Dr. Jonathan Silas Zwingina, who is also the Senate Deputy Leader, spoke with our correspondent, Matthew Onah in Yola, few minutes after picking his nomination form
Sir, you are the first candidate to pick the PDP governorship nomination form in Adamawa State, why the rush?

Original_Search_Title: SCOUL Can Look Elsewhere for Sugarcane Plantation!
Headline: Uganda: SCOUL Can Look Elsewhere for Sugarcane Plantation!
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE debate started by President Yoweri Museveni to degazett part of Mabira forest to SCOUL to boost sugar production, investment, jobs, and tax revenue should not be rested until consensus based on valid facts is reached.
While launching the Government Media Centre two months ago, Museveni said he had asked NEMA to give him unique benefits that Mabira adds to the environment so he could drop his proposal. Now, there have been very many commentaries from business, and ecological sides but the piece by Mr. Gaster Kiyingi, PR manager of the National Forestry Authority "Why emulate a cannibal who eats his children?" published on October 3 was excellent and informative, which I hope the president has read, and should be accepted.

Original_Search_Title: 'Grow Cassava in Drought Prone Areas'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Grow Cassava in Drought Prone Areas'
Body_Text:
Harare — FARMERS should grow cassava in drought prone areas where the crop complements maize and small grains.
Cassava farming has low production costs as it does not require a lot of fertilizer, grows well under high temperatures and poor soils but fetches more money on the market while it can also be used for domestic and industrial consumption.

Original_Search_Title: Loud Pat On the Back for Ministry of Health
Headline: Uganda: Loud Pat On the Back for Ministry of Health
Body_Text:
The Ministry of Health pulled off a major public relations coup this week when it ordered the Police to impound vehicles belonging to the ministry that were being used outside official business hours. By the end of the weekend the police had "harvested" eighty vehicles, some found loaded with charcoal, cassava tubers, beans still on their stems, live chicken and matooke among others.
To manage this highly commendable action, the Permanent Secretary Mr Muhammed Kezaala picked from the shelf a dusty government standing order issued by the Ministry of Public Service about three years ago to fight abuse of government vehicles.

Original_Search_Title: Mosaic Destroys Cassava in Lango
Headline: Uganda: Mosaic Destroys Cassava in Lango
Body_Text:
Kampala — CASSAVA mosaic has attacked cassava plants in Lango, causing concern among farmers over the fear of looming hunger.
Farmers in Lira, Apac, Oyam, Amolatar and Dokolo districts fear that the disease that has stunted the growth of cassava plants might result in famine.

Original_Search_Title: 13 Candidates to Challenge Incumbent President Ravalomanana in Polls
Headline: Madagascar: 13 Candidates to Challenge Ravalomanana in Madagascar Polls
Body_Text:
Madagascar will complete southern Africa's 2006 calendar for presidential elections with its 3 December poll that has attracted 13 challengers to face incumbent President Marc Ravalomanana.
Nominations for candidacy were confirmed by the country's constitutional high court on 19 October. Three other applicants were rejected.

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Classrooms Collapse
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO classroom blocks at different schools in Mukono district collapsed last week due to heavy rains, John Semakula reports.
The schools are Living Hope Nursery and Primary School in Nagojje sub-county and Kiwafu Primary School in Kimmenyedde sub-county.

Original_Search_Title: Dar Group Buys Mozambican Textile Factory
Headline: Tanzania: Dar Group Buys Mozambican Textile Factory
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A memorandum of understanding between IGEPE and MeTL was signed in April and later approved by the Mozambican government, writes FAUSTINE RWAMBALI One of the biggest business outfits in East Africa, Mohammed Enterprises Tanzania Ltd (MeTL) has acquired Texmoque, a giant textile factory in the northern Mozambican of Nampula.
The MeTL managing director, Mohammed Dewji told The EastAfrican last week that MeTL will invest $20 million in rehabilitation and modernisation of the textile factory in Mozambique, adding that production will resume by mid 2007 with a workforce of 800 people.

Original_Search_Title: Extending National Development Programmes
Headline: Nigeria: Extending National Development Programmes
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE ongoing national economic reforms are deficient because they do not go far enough to cover all the strategic areas of development and also because they have generated considerable job losses due to retrenchment of workers. Nigerians expect far reaching reforms which would affect other sectors such as the social, religious, judicial and political activities. Even the critical population factor has been left out.
Nigeria has always had a population problem defined as a situation in whch the population is growing faster than real resources. The quantitative aspect of the population problem is that with an assumed population growth rate of about three per cent, the Gross Domestic Product should be of the order of five to eight per cent. The rapid population growth implies that the average Nigeria will remain impoverished and also that some sectors of the economy will be adversely affected. Such sectors include agriculture, employment, education, health and housing. Two years ago, the federal government announced a new population policy.

Original_Search_Title: Staff Shortage Hampers Implementation of Women's Projects
Headline: Zimbabwe: Staff Shortage Hampers Implementation of Women's Projects
Body_Text:
Harare — AN ACUTE shortage of staff, coupled with inadequate funding and motor vehicles, among other required equipment, has hampered the effective implementation of various projects that had been lined up by the Ministry of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development.
The ministry has no support staff in various provinces and districts, yet it intends to engage in cassava, sugar, jatropha and poultry projects, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Redefining Populist Policies
Headline: Nigeria: Redefining Populist Policies
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a recent write up, Mr. Kayode Komolafe noted that: "one of the huge ironies of our time is that some significant steps being taken in the realm of governance are being eclipsed by politics of bitterness". He cited the goings-on at the centre as all pervading and giving the semblance of governance practically being at a stand still. Nothing can be farther from the truth. In actual fact important activities such as the public hearing of the Fiscal Responsibility Bill were going on. In short the politics of bitterness is threatening to engulf us as a nation and overshadow some of the benefits that have been clearly gained from some acts of good governance.
Indeed Komolafe's observation could also be said to be true in some states. High voltage pronouncements by some politicians seeking office in the next years' elections make it sound like the past three - and -a -half years have been for nothing and no gains have been made. In recent times the Ogun State Government has come under attack for what critics describe as non-performance. These pronouncements are based upon what the announcers regard as the non implementation of "populist programmes".

Original_Search_Title: Bayelsa, USAID, Shell to Raise $25 Million for N/Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Bayelsa, USAID, Shell to Raise $25 Million for N/Delta
Body_Text:
Abuja — Bayelsa State Government, United States Agency for International Development (USAID/Nigeria) and Shell Petroleum Development Company, would today launch a $25m partnership to improve living standard and social services in the Niger Delta region.
The partnership which is aimed at the people of the state and the Niger Delta would provide $25m for cassava production and processing, aquaculture value chain development, integrated HIV/AIDS service provision, and other development projects to be agreed upon by the partners. Of the total amount, about $10 million has been earmarked specifically for Bayelsa State residents with a view to improving their livelihoods and services available to them.

Original_Search_Title: We'll Meet Food Needs, Says Kirwa
Headline: Kenya: We'll Meet Food Needs, Says Kirwa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya will meet the Millennium Development Goal on food sufficiency, the Government has announced.
Agriculture minister, Mr Kipruto arap Kirwa said a raft of programmes were on course to meet the date of 2015.

Original_Search_Title: I Swear, I Didn't Betray My Boss, Says Akala
Headline: Nigeria: I Swear, I Didn't Betray My Boss, Says Akala
Body_Text:
Ibadan — After 9 months on the saddle as the Oyo state helmsman, controversy still trails the emergence of Otunba Christopher Adebayo Alao-Akala, erstwhile deputy to the impeached Governor of Oyo State Senator, Rashidi Ladoja. In an encounter with Ademola Adeyemo in Ibadan Akala explains the circumstances that surrounded the impeachment saga and how he emerged as 'governor'
Otunba Adebayo Alao Akala was at pains when he met with political journalists as he tried to explain the circumstances that led to his emergence as the 'Governor' of Oyo state following the controversial impeachment of his former boss Senator Rashidi Ladoja. Akala, a retired Senior Police Officer and former local government council chairman who said he was bombarded with barrages of questions over the moral implications of his ascendance to the office of the Governor said it was not the first time a governor would be removed and his deputy will step in. " I wondered why all these noise about me. There was nothing peculiar about my emergence as 'Governor' that should warrant all these attacks, Balarabe Musa was impeached in the second republic, " Akala explained that as deputy governor he was extremely loyal to his boss to the extent that he narrowly escaped death three times in the hands of those who planned Ladoja's removal.

Original_Search_Title: Chirano Mines Renege On Promises
Headline: Ghana: Chirano Mines Renege On Promises
Body_Text:
The Chiefs, Elders and members of Etwiebo, Akoti and the entire Chirano communities in the Bibiani-Anhiawso-Bekwai District of the Western Region have raised concerns about the operations of Chirano Gold Mines, an Australian gold mining company, in Ghana.
Speaking on behalf of the communities, the Abakumahene of Chirano, Nana Elue Yaw, told the press yesterday in Accra that the mining company has reneged on almost all its promises to the community since it started operations in the Etwiebo and Akoti area about a decade ago.

Original_Search_Title: South West - Tiko Mayor Acclaimed for Donating Farm Tools
Headline: Cameroon: South West - Tiko Mayor Acclaimed for Donating Farm Tools
Body_Text:
Mutengene, a road junction Town of Tiko Sub/Division was the theatre of agric excellence October 16. The South West Governor, Eyeya Zanga made one of his rare outings to preside at this year's joint event to mark the 11th Rural women s Day and the 26th World Food Day.
Women of the five zones of Tiko, Mutengene, Modika, Mondoni and Misselele came out en masse to show their abilities in food production. Cassava, Cocoyams, Yams, Plantains, Colocacia, maize and just any other food crop were displayed for grading and prizing. The Lebang women carried the day in Colocacia as best farmers of the specialty.

Original_Search_Title: Mama Tendo in Katanga Slum
Headline: Uganda: Mama Tendo in Katanga Slum
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Mama Tendo Foundation has started outreach seminars for people living in slums in Kampala. Until recently, the foundation targeted the educated public but is now spreading out to cover even the less privileged.
The seminars aim at educating the families on care during pregnancy, breastfeeding, nutrition and on how to raise children in the tough environments.

Original_Search_Title: Investing In Agriculture For Good Security
Headline: Liberia: Investing In Agriculture For Good Security
Body_Text:
This year's theme marking the 26th observance of World Food Day: "Investing in Agriculture for Food Security," as chosen by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), is extremely significant to the post-war environment in which the Liberian nation that had once striven along the lines of subsistence farming, watching limited production of crops but yet with farmers ever-prepared to accord relevant hospitality to others as a result of internal abundance, must equally conform to by virtue of the flaring rice-bird mentality that has again begun creeping in the post-election period.
Not viewed in the least sense of trying to win a prizes that do not suit the article, but primarily evolving from the continuous commitment of this writer who also possesses specialized knowledge in the field, it is only proper to critically review this year's theme within the post-war context of trying to help the reconstruction process as regards food security.

Original_Search_Title: Kayunga Farmer Turns to Pawpaw, Targets CHOGM
Headline: Uganda: Kayunga Farmer Turns to Pawpaw, Targets CHOGM
Body_Text:
Kampala — KANGULUMIRA sub-county in Kayunga district is well-known as pineapple land because pineapple seems to be a must-grow crop.
"Almost every household has grown the succulent fruit," says Ronald Kizito, 36.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Faces Obstacles
Headline: Namibia: Agriculture Faces Obstacles
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Although Namibia has a favourable policy environment for agricultural investment, many obstacles prevent small agricultural producers from producing a surplus apart from feeding themselves.
Lack of credit and markets, together with insecure land tenure, remain some of the hurdles that the small and emerging producers of agricultural products face, which President Hifikepunye Pohamba says is beyond their control.

Original_Search_Title: World Food Day Celebrated in South
Headline: Namibia: World Food Day Celebrated in South
Body_Text:
Mariental — More than 600 people from the community of Mariental, including teachers, learners, elders, regional councillors and Governor Katrina Hanse, gathered on Saturday at the Sportsground in commemoration of the 26th World Food Day (WFD) and the 14th International Day for the Eradication of Poverty (IDEP).
Under the theme: "Invest in Agriculture, For Food Security" the masses experienced an information and cultural extravaganza, with performances from schools, DD. Guibeb Primary and Mariental High. Empelheim illustrated diverse channelling of concern with a Tswana - and Gumboot dance accompanied by a powerful poem.

Original_Search_Title: CET Tariff Threatens 230,000 Jobs -- Ethanol Manufacturers
Headline: Nigeria: CET Tariff Threatens 230,000 Jobs -- Ethanol Manufacturers
Body_Text:
BARLEY few days after Ropes manufacturers petitioned the Presidency to reverse the recently hiked import duty on their raw materials by the Federal Ministry of Finance, which jerked it up from 5 per cent to 60 per cent, the Ethanol Manufacturers and Blenders group of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) has equally called to the Federal Government to review the new ECOWAS Common External Tariff (CET) on their excise duty and imported packaging materials saying that the CET scheme threatens the N8 billion ethanol plant under construction, and the over 230,000 jobs in the sector.
Addressing newsmen in Lagos on the impact of the new CET, Chairman of the sector, Mr. Joseph Odumodu, said: "We wish to draw the attention of Government and relevant Agencies to the recent amendment to the import duty for bottled wines and spirits (finished products), which slashed tariff from 80 per cent to 20 per cent (Whisky wine), 90 per cent to 20 per cent for Sparkling wine, Varamouth 90 per cent to 20 per cent and others reduced from 70 per cent to 20 per cent, while local manufacturers are required to pay 40 per cent excise duty in addition to 50 per cent on their imported packaging materials ."

Original_Search_Title: Farm Project Helps Aids Orphans Get Food, Income
Headline: Kenya: Farm Project Helps Aids Orphans Get Food, Income
Body_Text:
Bondo — A UN project is helping to sustain farming know-how and other life skills among Kenyan children orphaned by AIDS.
The Junior Farmer Field and Life Schools project (JFFLS), which has been running for two years, teaches basic farming techniques and social health, including family planning, alcohol abuse and gender equality, to 120 orphans at four primary schools in the village of Odhuro in the district of Bondo in western Kenya's Nyanza province.

Original_Search_Title: Green Revolution?
Headline: Africa: Green Revolution?
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — The Gates Foundation has joined with the Rockefeller Foundation in promoting a new "Green Revolution" in Africa. But will the new effort learn from the mistakes of earlier "Green Revolution" initiatives? Sceptics say that the new proposals still disregard the interests of small farmers and the environment.
This AfricaFocus Bulletin contains a press release from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation announcing the new initiative, and a critique from GRAIN, an NGO that promotes sustainable management of agricultural diversity. Another AfricaFocus Bulletin sent out today examines the case of African rice development in particular, which proponents say is one Green Revolution initiative that does incorporate participation of small farmers and promotes biodiversity.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Moses Makayoto - Award Winning Innovator
Headline: Kenya: Dr. Moses Makayoto - Award Winning Innovator
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Dr Moses Makayoto may not easily ring a bell as a top civil servant but his papers and innovations have given him global recognition across the oceans.
He was the brain behind the popular Mama Safi detergent, which you could not miss in many households in the 1980s.

Original_Search_Title: Elephants Invade Farms In Lofa - Destroy Several Crops
Headline: Liberia: Elephants Invade Farms In Lofa - Destroy Several Crops
Body_Text:
Reports emanating from Lofa County speak of elephants invading villages and farms, as well as destroying crops before returning into the forest.
Although the situation is being investigated, several aid workers and residents of the county who claim to have returned from some of the affected villages and towns, told The Analyst in Monrovia that the repeated attacks on crops by the animals have caused massive displacement of villagers in several villages around the provincial city of Voinjama.

Original_Search_Title: Ukrainians Are Not Rude to Nigerians -Ambassador
Headline: Nigeria: Ukrainians Are Not Rude to Nigerians -Ambassador
Body_Text:
Recently, the Ukrainian Ambassador to Nigeria, Oleg Skoropad, and members of the Ukrainian community in Abuja celebrated their 15th Independence Anniversary, bringing Ukraine's relations with Nigeria into lime light. He represents a country that has a growing economic relationship with Nigeria with a turn over of $225 million in 2005. Speaking with our reporter, Elk-anah Chawai, he bares his mind on the nature of Nigeria-Ukraine political and economic relations. He also reacts to allegations that Ukrainians are rude to Nigerians at their embassy. The excerpts:
Tell us about yourself

Original_Search_Title: Budget of Consolidation
Headline: Nigeria: Budget of Consolidation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Being text of the 2007 budget speech presented by President Olusegun Obasanjo at the joint session of the National Assembly in Abuja on Wednesday
It is with great honour that I present to this Joint Session of the National Assembly, the 2007 Budget Proposal of the Federal Government of Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: FG Presents N2.3 Trillion Appropriation Bill
Headline: Nigeria: FG Presents N2.3 Trillion Appropriation Bill
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is with great honour that I present to this Joint Session of the National Assembly, the 2007 Budget Proposal of the Federal Government of Nigeria.
As with the budgets for the last three years, the 2007 budget has been prepared against the background of a Medium-Term Expenditure Framework, which takes account of forecast revenue and expenditure for the next three years.

Original_Search_Title: Among Oil Men
Headline: Nigeria: Among Oil Men
Body_Text:
Over three months ago, I received an invitation from the Nigerian Acting Secretary General of the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), Malam Mohammed S. Barkindo, to attend the 3rd OPEC International Seminar in Vienna, Austria, the organisation's headquarters. The two-day seminar billed for September 12 and 13 had the interesting theme of the challenges and opportunities OPEC would face in today's so-called New Energy Era.
Needless to say, it was an invitation that I certainly could not decline. First, it was an all expenses paid invitation to one of the most historic and also one of the most cosmopolitan cities in the world, hosting, as it were, several international organisations and agencies. Second, oil, the commodity to be discussed, is an exciting topic any day.

Original_Search_Title: Foreign Reserve Now Above $40 Billion
Headline: Nigeria: Foreign Reserve Now Above $40 Billion
Body_Text:
Abuja — Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Prof Charles Soludo, yesterday disclosed that the country's external reserves is now slightly above $40 billion.
But Lagos State Governor, Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu, is not very comfortable with the news saying, rather than accumulate reserves and keep them in foreign accounts, the Federal Government should release the monies to the three tiers of government for developmental projects and programmes that would pull the ordinary people out of poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Ibori, Uduaghan And the Delta Dream
Headline: Nigeria: Ibori, Uduaghan And the Delta Dream
Body_Text:
GIVEN the current level of human and capital development in the Niger-Delta region and most especially in Delta State, which gets the highest allocation from FG, getting the top of the MDGs by 2015 will need more than just business as usual come 2007. The dream of most Deltans is to have a state that they can boast of, a state where things work for real in a commensurable level with the huge allocation it gets from the Federal Government and internally generated income. To do this, the state must come up with a man of greater vision and determination who can bring to pass the dream of Delta State - state of Uhuru.
Studying the prevailing political atmosphere in respect of the forthcoming governorship election in the state where there are many hopefuls gunning for the promising governorship seat, it is time the people focus their mind on the best candidate that can deliver, bring their dreams and aspiration as state and people to fruition. As Moses led his people to the promised land, so the people of Delta need a man of timber and calibre.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Cause Food Shortage in Mukono
Headline: Uganda: Rains Cause Food Shortage in Mukono
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS prayed and even fasted for it. Their prayers were answered. It came, and in abundance. Now the rains have left plantations in Mukono district in ruins.
"I sell plantain for a living, but at this rate, I am afraid I may not have a good harvest. The thunderstorm has destroyed my plantation," says Settube, a farmer in Upper Kauga village in Mukono town council.

Original_Search_Title: ICT Boosts Farmers' Profits
Headline: Uganda: ICT Boosts Farmers' Profits
Body_Text:
Kampala — Teo Kamya, a farmer of Sango parish in Mpigi district, no longer needs to go to Kampala to look for market for her produce.
With a village phone at her residence, she only needs to call and know about the market prices for her red pepper, tomatoes, potatoes, cassava, bananas and milk.

Original_Search_Title: ICT Boosts Farmers' Profits
Headline: Uganda: ICT Boosts Farmers' Profits
Body_Text:
Kampala — BUSINESS: Farmers can use village phones to find out market prices
Teo Kamya, a farmer of Sango parish in Mpigi district, no longer needs to go to Kampala to look for market for her produce.

Original_Search_Title: Mayor Calls Religious Leaders On Poverty
Headline: Rwanda: Mayor Calls Religious Leaders On Poverty
Body_Text:
Kigali — The Mayor of Bugesera District, Gaspard Musonera has called on all religious leaders in the district to engage in poverty reduction projects to wipe out famine in the area. The Mayor revealed this on October 6, during a ceremony to launch a Catholic Relief project (CARTAS) aimed at increasing food production and reducing poverty in the area, held in Nyamata Sector.
He advised the project implementers to work hand in hand with agricultural officers at the sector level to promote crops favourable to the climate in the district.

Original_Search_Title: As NDC Presidential Race Gets Murkie, Ayariga Nails Spio, Annan
Headline: Ghana: As NDC Presidential Race Gets Murkie, Ayariga Nails Spio, Annan
Body_Text:
Mr. Mahama Ayariga, the 32-year-old legislator and Spokesperson for Prof. John Evans Attah Mills, former Vice President and leading contender in the race for the presidential ticket of the National Democratic Congress (NDC) has condemned the campaign strategies of Dr. Ekwow Spio-Garbrah and Mr. Eddie Annan, who are also battling for the party's presidential ticket.
In an interview with The Chronicle yesterday, the young lawmaker who had to resist pressures from his party leadership and risked losing his respected Parliamentary position as Minority Spokesperson on Legal and Constitutional Affairs, to hold on to the new position as spokesperson to the Professor, said the campaign strategies of the two contestants, whom he saw as underdogs, were unhealthy for the party.

Original_Search_Title: Veterans Held Over Wetland
Headline: Uganda: Veterans Held Over Wetland
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police on Thursday arrested six veterans for cutting down a forest. The six, members of Muduuma Veterans association (MVA), cut down five hectares of natural forest at Lwamunda Central Forestry Reserve (CFR).
Lwamunda CFR, which has 4,696 hectares of forestland, is located in Nalya-Nasese village, Muduuma sub-county, Mpigi district.

Original_Search_Title: Ibori's Successor - Matters Arising
Headline: Nigeria: Ibori's Successor - Matters Arising
Body_Text:
Asaba — THE matter of succession in Delta State is becoming unique in several ways. For one, Governor James Ibori has not indicated interest in vying for the nation's presidency like most of his colleagues who have fallen in love with klieg lights. He has not joined the bandwagon and has remained studiously silent on his political future.
This could lead one into believing that he could be reasonably distant from the din of the clamour for his successor. He has already indicated that he would throw his massive political weight behind anyone that his party - the PDP- throws up as its candidate for the 2007 gubernatorial election. That is yet another good score as some of his colleagues have become campaign managers of some aspirants in their respective states.

Original_Search_Title: Horsfall Tasks N/Delta Governors On Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Horsfall Tasks N/Delta Governors On Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos — Adviser to President Olusegun Obasanjo on the Marshal Plan for the Niger Delta, Chief A.K. Horsfall, has called on state governments in the region to give more responsibilities and resources to the actual communities affected by oil exploitation for their own development as such communities know their own priorities.
Horsfall, currently on a week-long projects monitoring visit to Ondo State made the call while inspecting various completed and on-going projects being implemented by service providers in Ondo State, including Ondo State government, Ondo State Oil Producing Areas Development Commission (OSOPADEC), Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC), Owena River Basin Authority and the Federal Ministry of Communications.

Original_Search_Title: Mobile Phone Purchases Rise
Headline: Ghana: Mobile Phone Purchases Rise
Body_Text:
THE ACTING Government Statistician, Prof. N. Nsowah Nuamah, has emphasized that Ghanaians are buying more mobile phones in the country than ever; hence contributing to stable prices of food items in the basket for measuring the Consumer Price Index (CPI).
This, he said, has helped in the current price stability of foodstuffs in the markets, inasmuch as excess expenditure on food alone in an economy was never a good sign.

Original_Search_Title: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Headline: Nigeria: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Today, in the ancient town of Owu, in Ogun State, Nasarawa State governor, Alhaji Abdulahi Adamu, will add another feather to his cap. He will be conferred with the chieftaincy title of Teruru of Owu kingdom by the Olowu of Owu, Oba Dosumu.
This conferment will mark the second time that the governor will bag chieftaincy awards in the South- west of the country. Only in May last year, Adamu was conferred with the title of Aare Obateru of Ile-Ife by the Ooni of Ife, Oba Okunade Sijuade.

Original_Search_Title: Gani Says Obasanjo's Shares Breached Code of Conduct
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Shares - Gani Drags
Body_Text:
Lagos — Frontline lawyer, Chief Gani Fawehinmi (SAN), yesterday dragged President Olusegun Obasanjo before the Code of Conduct Bureau in Abuja, asking it to charge the President to the Code of Conduct Tribunal for alleged contravention of Paragraph 13, Part 1 of the Fifth Schedule of the 1999 Constitution.
Specifically, the learned senior advocate said the President's acquisition of shares in a mega corporation, Trans-National Corporation (Transcorp) Plc, through Obasanjo Holdings Limited OHL), breached the code of conduct for public officers.

Original_Search_Title: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Headline: Nigeria: Again, Honour Beckons On Abdullahi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Today, in the ancient town of Owu, in Ogun State, Nasarawa State governor, Alhaji Abdulahi Adamu, will add another feather to his cap. He will be conferred with the chieftaincy title of Teruru of Owu kingdom by the Olowu of Owu, Oba Dosumu.
This conferment will mark the second time that the governor will bag chieftaincy awards in the South- west of the country. Only in May last year, Adamu was conferred with the title of Aare Obateru of Ile-Ife by the Ooni of Ife, Oba Okunade Sijuade.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Falls to 6 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE annual headline inflation rate for September dropped to 6.2% from 7.5% in August.
"Prices of foods like sweet potatoes and cassava went up by 1.7%, while matooke prices went up in Kampala, Jinja, Mbale, Masaka and Mbarara except in Gulu. Fish, milk and eggs' prices increased in a number of centres due to seasonal and distribution factors. Prices of fruits, some vegetables and cereals went up due to low supply," Steven Bahemuka, the acting principal statistician at the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), said.

Original_Search_Title: Nostalgia
Headline: Nigeria: Nostalgia
Body_Text:
Independence anniversary celebration inadvertently brings the subject of history to the centre stage, which at least for once challenges the daily dictatorship and pre-eminence of the twin-subject of economics and politics in our lives.
This year's 46th independence anniversary was no exception. The celebration was officially declared "low-key" but not without another generous official public holiday (on a Monday 2nd October!) to enable us certainly deepen our idle and unproductive capacity; further entrench poverty and illiteracy (schools also close today!).

Original_Search_Title: Bauchi to Establish Electronics Coy
Headline: Nigeria: Bauchi to Establish Electronics Coy
Body_Text:
Bauchi State has concluded arrangements to establish an electrical and electronics company to stimulate industrialisation.
Gov. Ahmadu Mu'azu made this known Friday in Bauchi while declaring open the 17th Meeting of the Nati-onal Council on Industry.

Original_Search_Title: Council to Commercialise Plants in Kaduna, Kano, Katsina
Headline: Nigeria: Council to Commercialise Plants in Kaduna, Kano, Katsina
Body_Text:
The Kaolin Beneficiation plants in Katsina and Kano States, Phosphate Beneficiation and Intergraded Groundnut oil mill, Sokoto state, Granulated Limestone, Calabar and fruit juice processing plant in Kaduna State are to be commercialised by the Raw Materials Research and Development Council {RMRDC}.
According to the Dire-ctor General of the Council, Professor Azikiwe Peter Onwualu arrangements to divest from these plants have since commenced.

Original_Search_Title: Kasubi Market
Headline: Uganda: Kasubi Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — The moon is shining brightly as energetic men offload trucks of produce. It is Friday night and tomorrow is the big market day, so naturally it's a night out - only in Kasubi, instead of beer sipping, the overnight activity is the offloading.
The market looks beautiful, lit as it is by countless paraffin candles (tadooba), each announcing the location of a trader.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo - Time to Reject Pseudo Leaders
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo - Time to Reject Pseudo Leaders
Body_Text:
Broadcast by His Excellency, President Olusegun Obasanjo, GCFR on the occasion of the 46th Independence Anniversary ofThe Federal Republic of Nigeria Abuja, October 1, 2006
MY dear compatriots,

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Production - Ihonvbere Warns Against Glut in Global Market
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Production - Ihonvbere Warns Against Glut in Global Market
Body_Text:
Benin — AS the demand for Nigeria cassava in the international market increases, the Special Adviser on Project Monitoring and Evaluation to President Obasanjo, Prof. Julius Ihonvbere has cautioned against a situation where the rush for cassava export could cause a glut in the global market, especially China which is Nigeria's target export market.
Professor Ihonvbere who was represented by Dr. Tunde Lakoju at a two-day workshop on cassava production organised by a Benin-base Co-operative Society, Out Eyotor, specifically called on Government Agencies to properly guide cassava farmers on how to export the root crop to avoid a glut, even as he called for processing cassava to its different alternatives locally.

Original_Search_Title: FG Approves Imo, Abia for NDDC Intervention
Headline: Nigeria: FG Approves Imo, Abia for NDDC Intervention
Body_Text:
Owerri — THE Federal Government has approved the accommodation of Imo and Abia States in the presidential intervention in the coastal areas under the aegis of the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC).
Announcing this in Owerri, after presenting 50 pieces of 300KVA transformers and refuse trucks to Imo State government, the NDDC Chairman, Ambassador Sam Edem, said the organisation would soon communicate the approval to the governors concerned."

Original_Search_Title: 2007 - I Can Lead Delta State - Okowa
Headline: Nigeria: 2007 - I Can Lead Delta State - Okowa
Body_Text:
Asaba — Dr. Ifeanyi Okowa is the Delta State Commissioner for Health. He has served the Ibori administration in several capacities since 1999. He spoke on the Ibori administration since 1999, his stewardship and his readiness to lead the state when called upon. He spoke to Kingsley Nwezeh in Asaba.
The issue in Delta politics as we approach the 2007 general elections is the issue of power shift. How would you react to the cry of marginalization by some sections of the state?

Original_Search_Title: 'Agriculture'll Be My Priority'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Agriculture'll Be My Priority'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Godson Echegile, one of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)'s gubernatorial aspirants in Delta State, yesterday said the state will become a front-runner in agricultural transformation next year.
Echegile said this in an interactive session with agriculture experts in Asaba, adding that he will partner with local and foreign investors to trigger the oil palm sector into an engine of the economic growth through oil palm enhancement programme.

Original_Search_Title: Ashanti Region, Potential Business Hub of Ghana
Headline: Ghana: Ashanti Region, Potential Business Hub of Ghana
Body_Text:
Ashanti Region, located within the middle portion of the country and one of Ghana's fastest growing regions, has in diverse ways contributed to the development of Ghana.
The Ashanti region is not only known for its cultural, agricultural and tourism prospects, but also its capital, Kumasi, is the second biggest city in Ghana and also considered as the Garden City of West Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Uganda Loses 11 Percent of Produced Cassava
Headline: Uganda Loses 11 Percent of Produced Cassava
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA losses 11% (590,000 metric tonnes) of cassava production per annum to waste out of an estimated 5,373,000 metric tonnes. According to the World Food Programme balance sheet, 2002, 41% is utilised for animal and poultry feeds, while 48% goes for human food. Patrick Kalunda, the post-harvest officer, said this during a workshop at Jokas Hotel in Bweyogerere.
Research shows increased production, but farmers are still constrained with low incomes from cassava that could be attributed to pre- and post-harvest losses, fluctuating prices, poor marketing, lack of knowledge, low processing and demand.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Marketing
Headline: Zambia: Crop Marketing
Body_Text:
There have been concerns about how the crop marketing exercise has been handled by the Food Reserve Agency (FRA) and clearly many farmers are not pleased with the whole exercise.
Firstly, some farmers are worried whether their crop will be bought at all because they are unable to access some of the depots including the satellite depots that the FRA has opened.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Headline: Nigeria: UN Agency Challenges Nigeria On Industrial Development
Body_Text:
An agency of the United Nations at the weekend in Abuja challenged captains of Nigerian economy to steer the country's economy towards independent of international support for industrial development.
Speaking at the occasion of signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with African Business Round Table (ABR) on one hand and the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) on the other, the Director General of UNIDO in the West African subregion, Dr.Kandel Yumkella said: "No UN agency can help Nigerians to develop the industrial base of their economy. The people need to wake up to the reality of the challenges facing them. The government needs to create enabling environment for private participation in the industrial development of the country for the sake of creating jobs for the mass of jobless youth.

Original_Search_Title: Alima Mahama - Government is Doing a Lot Toward Women Empowerment
Headline: Ghana: Alima Mahama - Government is Doing a Lot Toward Women Empowerment
Body_Text:
Various women groupings countrywide have benefited from the disbursement of a 124 billion cedis facility accruing from the establishment of the Women Development Fund, Hajia Alima Mahama, Minister for Women and Children's Affairs (MOWAC) has said.
She said the Fund has helped to generate employment, improved food security and reduced poverty among the womenfolk, especially those living in the rural areas.

Original_Search_Title: Tortuous Trek Up to Crash Site
Headline: Nigeria: Tortuous Trek Up to Crash Site
Body_Text:
Kaduna — When Weekly Trust got to the village of Ticha Mbaper and asked the youths who took part in the rescue with the youths of Mbakunu to take us to the site of the crash. We got on motorcycles from the village and drove to the foot of the mountain. Aondasee Gbaa, 20, who was also among the rescue team pointed up ahead at the peak of the mountain and asked if we thought we could manage the climb. He said it was a three hour climb if we made it fast without taking much rest on the way.
The climb up the mountain was in three phases. On the first phase of the climb we encountered several farmlands dotted all around the vicinity with thick foliages of trees further adding darkness all around us. While on our way we met farmers sitting on yam ridges holding some parts of the crashed plane. Aondaasee pointed at the shafts and metals they are holding and said: "See, people are already going up to scavenge for parts of the plane. They will go to town and display it to curious sightseers who have been trooping to this area. They hope to make money out of the show."

Original_Search_Title: Transcorp Shares - Why Obasanjo Must Go
Headline: Nigeria: Transcorp Shares - Why Obasanjo Must Go
Body_Text:
Lagos — The following is the full text of the press statement issued by Chief Gani Fawehinmi LL.D, SAN on the Constitutional implications of the part- ownership of Transcorp by President Olusegun Obasanjo.
In the last one week, facts have emerged that the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, General Olusegun Obasanjo substantially owns, through Obasanjo Holdings Limited, Transnational Corporation of Nigeria Plc (TRANSCORP).

Original_Search_Title: WFP to Open Mill to Process Zambian-Grown Cassava
Headline: Zambia: WFP to Open Mill to Process Zambian-Grown Cassava
Body_Text:
Lusaka — The United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) has announced it is setting up a cassava milling plant in northern Zambia, to facilitate the processing of locally purchased cassava to feed the 69,000 refugees currently being assisted by WFP in the country.
By early 2007, WFP will purchase 250 metric tons of cassava from local farmers every month, to process as fortified flour for the refugees.

Original_Search_Title: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Headline: Nigeria: War Against Fake Drugs . . . So Far So What?
Body_Text:
Lagos — The five-year leadership of the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) received another round of accolades recently at the public presentation of a report on the level of counterfeit and substandard medicines in Nigeria. It was sponsored by the World Health Organisation (WHO) and the British Department for International Development (DFID).
And as usual, it was another round of celebration for an agency that has done so well on the local scene that the international arena is always eager to share in its success. The source of its celebration came from the report itself, which says that the level of counterfeit drugs in the country has dropped from a staggering average of 41 per cent in 2001 to 16.7 per cent this year. But the country representative of DFID in Nigeria, Dr. Cassidy, aptly expressed the caveat on this celebration when he said that NAFDAC must not relent in its war against counterfeit and sub-standard products.

Original_Search_Title: Questions Over Obasanjo's Shares in Transcorp
Headline: Nigeria: Questions Over Obasanjo's Shares in Transcorp
Body_Text:
Last week Wednesday, Chairman of Transnational Corporation, Dr. Ndi Okereke-Onyuike confirmed speculations that President Olusegun Obasanjo holds equity shares in the company. Her revelation came barely 48 hours after the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) raided the Head Office of the company reportedly in a bid to 'investigate' the shareholding structure and operations of the company.
Okereke-Onyuike told the House Committee on Capital Markets that the president had subscribed to the shares of the company when it was established. Even though she did not specify the amount of shares held by the president, her revelation confirmed media reports that the president owns between 200million to 600million shares in Transcorp. Although throughout the nearly two years of its existence, there have been strong speculations that the president had significant shares in the company, no concrete evidence had been brought forward to substantiate the claim until about a month ago when it was reported in the papers that the president had between 200million to 600million shares being held in 'blind trust' in the corporation.

Original_Search_Title: Emulating Gani Audu
Headline: Nigeria: Emulating Gani Audu
Body_Text:
THE setting was grand for the Award of Excellence to the best 30 local government chairmen in Nigeria by the management of Daily Independent newspaper, which includes who's who in Nigerian politics at the Shehu Musa Yar'Adu Centre, Abuja.
With the calm weather and friendly atmosphere walked in a friend of mine Professor Godwin Sogolo, formerly of the Institute for Peace and Conflict Resolution Abuja, whom I have lost contact with for some time. As we were exchanging pleasantries came in Mr. Ted Ewere, Editor-in-Chief of Daily Independent newspaper. Seeing these mighty men of the pen was an indication that something serious was in the making. In no time there was an unusual gathering of local government chairmen from different political parties complete with posters indicative of success. Hearing about the event ignited my curiosity as I was informed that the occasion was a meirt award night for the best 30 local government elected chairmen in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Only Justice And Equity'll Make Democracy Work
Headline: Nigeria: Only Justice And Equity'll Make Democracy Work
Body_Text:
The Chinua Achebe Foundation Interview Series
Disclaimer: The views expressed in the interview are not necessarily those of the Chinua Achebe Foundation. The Chinua Achebe Foundation, an intellectual and cultural organisation, believes in the right of every Nigerian to express their opinion.

Original_Search_Title: Country Turns to Cassava for Energy
Headline: Tanzania: Country Turns to Cassava for Energy
Body_Text:
Dar Es Salaam — Millions of dollars being used now for the importation of petroleum products could be saved, thanks to an ambitious three-year pilot project on the use of cassava to produce energy sufficient enough to drive machines.
The project is run by the Tanzania Commission for Science and Technology (COSTECH).

Original_Search_Title: The Bicycle Has Transformed the Lives of Many Ordinary Ugandans
Headline: Uganda: The Bicycle Has Transformed the Lives of Many Ordinary Ugandans
Body_Text:
The bicycle invented in 1816 has transformed the lives of many Ugandans today. Some people say the bicycle is their lifeline, without it their future would be bleak.Thanks to Baron von Drais who invented it well before the 19th century.
When in 1816 Baron Karl von Drais, a German, invented the bicycle, perhaps none thought it could turn out to be a source of livelihood for countless people especially in the developing world.

Original_Search_Title: This is Where I Want to Die
Headline: Nigeria: This is Where I Want to Die
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Henry Allam was born in Ibadan 74 years ago. He has since remained in the country. His wife, Margot, though born in Lebanon, was brought to Nigeria at the age of 10 in 1953. She too has since remained here. His children, three of them, all have the same ambition-to live their entire lives in Nigeria. The eldest, Robert, who runs the family business, hopes to become the governor of Oyo state someday. His second child, Isabelle, is married to a man who though is also of Lebanese origin, was born in Lagos. The man, who runs an eye-brow restaurant in Lagos says he dreads the thought of traveling out of this country, even for a week holiday. Thrice ( after much pressure from his children) he attempted taking a short vacation outside the country but each time, made a detour on airport road and drove back to his home on Victoria Island where he is landlord, at different locations, to many Nigerians.
The last one, Soraya, who is temporarily in Saudi Arabia on official posting met her man in Lagos where they have both pitched their tent. Growing up in the old Gbagi, popularly known as Lebanon street in Ibadan in the midst of friends and school mates at Ibadan Boys High School, shuttling from one party and cinema house to another at night and visiting exotic restaurants, swimming pools and amusement parks in the day, before coming down to Lagos to start business where those things continued unabated, Henry, the third child of five, has enjoyed this one life. This he admits gleefully.

Original_Search_Title: Banks Told to Review Lending Requirements
Headline: Nigeria: Banks Told to Review Lending Requirements
Body_Text:
Abuja — Banks in the country have been told to review their stringent lending requirements in order to improve access to development funds. This call was contained in a communiqué issued by the National Council on Industry after its two-day meeting, which held in Bauchi, Bauchi State recently.
It would be recalled that the Central Bank of Nigeria, in its effort to stimulate the growth of small-scale business in the country, directed all banks to keep 10 per cent of their profit after tax in a reserve from which credit will be given to small-scale entrepreneurs. Nothwithstanding, entrepreneurs still find it difficult to access these funds because of the conditions and rates attached to them, while the banks blame lack of good business ideas and commitments on the part of the entrepreneurs for the hitch in accessing the reserves.

Original_Search_Title: As Casamance Fighting Continues, Displaced Bed in
Headline: Senegal: As Casamance Fighting Continues, Displaced Bed in
Body_Text:
Sindian — Thousands of people who have fled fighting between the Senegal army and pro-independence rebels in Senegal's southern Casamance region are bedding in for a long wait to go home.
Fighting in the region started on 16 August, when the Senegalese army started moving north from a base at Sindian in Casamance, locals say. After an initial week of heavy fighting there was a lull until 24 August when more fighting was heard.

Original_Search_Title: Do We Really Need Speed Governors?
Headline: Uganda: Do We Really Need Speed Governors?
Body_Text:
My friends, things are indeed getting complicated in the city. First, we had the mayor picking a fight with a man who knows more about fighting than anyone else in this country.
Then on Monday, on my way to work, I found the police had staged a big operation and they were impounding taxis. I later learnt that this was because they didn't have speed governors.

Original_Search_Title: Proposed Law Could Outlaw Female Genital Mutilation
Headline: Guinea Bissau: Proposed Law Could Outlaw Female Genital Mutilation
Body_Text:
Bafata — The practice of fanado, or female genital mutilation, could soon be outlawed by a new bill to be presented to the Guinea-Bissau parliament.
Fanado is a traditional initiation ceremony for young girls, in which the vagina's clitoris and lips are removed. Many of the country's 30 ethnic groups practice it, including all the Muslim communities, about 46 percent of the population, especially in the eastern regions of Gabu and Bafatá.

Original_Search_Title: Newspaper Editor Harassed, Chased By Guards of Suspected Drug Criminal
Headline: Ghana: Newspaper Editor Harassed, Chased By Guards of Suspected Drug Criminal
Body_Text:
On 12 September 2006, Andrew Edwin Arthur, editor of 'The Independent", a tri-weekly newspaper in Accra, was chased by security personnel guarding the confiscated residence of Kwabena Amaning, a suspected drug criminal who is on remand for drug related offences.
Arthur was chased away while taking a photograph of the confiscated mansion at East Legon, a suburb of Accra. The Serious Fraud Office (SFO), a state investigatory body, announced on 8 September the freezing of the assets of 21 individuals who are being investigated for narcotics-related offences.

Original_Search_Title: Africa Seeks Partnerships With Industrialising Nations
Headline: Africa Seeks Partnerships With Industrialising Nations
Body_Text:
The African Union hopes to speed Africa's path to industrialisation through partnerships with emerging powers in the South, such as Brazil, China, India and Venezuela.
Plans for the strategic partnerships are being announced this week at a meeting in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia held from 11 to 13 September.

Original_Search_Title: Food Production Declines As Angolan Refugees Go Home
Headline: Zambia: Food Production Declines As Angolan Refugees Go Home
Body_Text:
Solwezi — The repatriation of Angolan refugees is creating food shortages in and around the Zambian camps they have lived in for decades.
"The repatriation of Angolan refugees has affected us so much. They were very hardworking farmers and we used to buy cheap beans, rice, cassava, sweet potatoes and maize [the staple crop] from them, but now we have nowhere to run to for cheap foods," said village headman Chiwevu, whose hamlet is located on the outskirts of the Meheba refugee settlement in northwestern Zambia.

Original_Search_Title: Doubts Over Cassava Project
Headline: East Africa: Doubts Over Cassava Project
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Researchers have admitted that varieties of the genetically-modified cassava that they had declared to be disease-resistant were actually vulnerable to the devastating cassava mosaic disease. Dagi Kimani reports
CONTROVERSY HAS deepened over a multi-million dollar USAid-supported cassava research programme, which proponents had said would help boost East Africa's food security, but which critics critics have dismissed as an attempt by the United States to develop alternative sources of "renewable" energy.

Original_Search_Title: PRP Philosophy Drives My Politics - Achike Udenwa
Headline: Nigeria: PRP Philosophy Drives My Politics - Achike Udenwa
Body_Text:
Governor Achike Udenwa of Imo State is one of the People's Democratic Party (PDP) governors who have signified their intentions to seek the Party's ticket for the presidency in April 2007. After a most edifying talk on Democracy and Sustenance of Good Governance, which the American Consul-General in Nigeria, Mr. Brian Browne, gave in Owerri on Monday, August 28th, 2006 at the instance of the state government, Governor Udenwa sat down for this interview with OCHEREOME NNANNA and a couple of other editors invited to the occasion. Here are excerpts of the interaction:
What is the big connection between you and the American Consul-General, who has been here twice within a year? What is the basis of the relationship?

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Ethanol for Nigerian Cars Ready Soon - Kupolokun
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Ethanol for Nigerian Cars Ready Soon - Kupolokun
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has announced its readiness to start blending Premium Light Spirit (PMS) or Petrol used in Nigeria with 10% ethanol very soon, explaining its eight months' delay to the takeoff of the initiative is due to the unforeseen wide scope of work on the receiving facilities.
Speaking at the signing of a Cassava Research Memoran-dum Of Understanding (MoU) between the NNPC and the Federal University of Agriculture, Markudi, in Abuja yesterday, the Group Managing Director (GMD) of the NNPC, Engr. Funsho Kupolokun, told the gathering that the delay in the pilot importation of the ethanol fuel was as a result of the large scope of work at the receiving terminals at Atlas Cove and Mosimi in Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Expert Raises Hope On Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Expert Raises Hope On Cassava Production
Body_Text:
GOING by the tempo of research at the National Root Crops Research Institute (NRCRI) Umudike, Abia State, Nigeria will in the next five years, be producing eighty metric tons of cassava per hectare of farmland to meet the international demand for starch, the executive director of NRCRI, Dr Kenneth Nwosu has said.
At present, the country produces 38 million metric tons per annum which places her as the world's highest producer of the crop.

Original_Search_Title: Increased Production of Main Food Crops
Headline: Mozambique: Increased Production of Main Food Crops
Body_Text:
Maputo — This year's harvest in Mozambique has seen a considerable increase in the production of grains, pulses and cassava, according to the latest report from the Agriculture Ministry.
The report puts this year's production of grain at 2.09 million tonnes, an increase of 10 per cent on the 2005 figure.

Original_Search_Title: President Obasanjo Says Ethanol Will Be Compulsory for Nigerian Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: President Obasanjo Says Ethanol Will Be Compulsory for Nigerian Fuel
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo has said the Administration would make the use of ethanol in fuel compulsory, like the cassava flour in wheat for bread.
The President was speaking to Mr. Roberto Gianneti da Fonseca, Director of the Sao Paulo Federation of Industries, at State House, Abuja on September 7.

Original_Search_Title: Export And Tax Waivers
Headline: Nigeria: Export And Tax Waivers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ordinarily, the plan by the Federal Government to exempt some 33 products from export taxes would have been hailed as a sound economic decision to boost export trade and redirect attention from crude oil as the only foreign exchange earner. But the reality is that waiving export tariffs alone will not jump-start export initiatives among Nigerian entrepreneurs.
The government, under the trade initiative tagged "Commerce 33," had announced tax waivers for some 11 solid minerals (iron-ore, coal, zinc, tin); 11 agro-produce (cotton, cassava, cocoa, cashew, gum-arabic, fruits vegetables.) and 11 locally-manufactured items like bottled water, beverage, and soft drinks. The taxes removed include one percent consultancy charge, N25,000 NAFDAC levy for each container, and 11 percent container charges.

Original_Search_Title: A Night With IDPs
Headline: Uganda: A Night With IDPs
Body_Text:
Opit, Gulu — The bonfire cast the shadows of dozens of people against the walls of mud and wattle huts in the sprawling internally displaced persons' (IDPs) camp.
"The bonfire was the place where elders, the youth and women met to discuss solutions to problems facing the community," Milton Munu, an elder at Opit IDP camp in northern Uganda's Gulu District told the gathering. "But similar meetings were last held here 20 years ago. Because of insecurity, meetings around the bonfire that used to help the community close ranks have not been held since."

Original_Search_Title: Kyambogo's Bio-Ethanol Fuel Research Promising
Headline: Uganda: Kyambogo's Bio-Ethanol Fuel Research Promising
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE recent bio-ethanol fuel tests done by Dr. Bagenda Ssengonzi, the head of Kyambogo University Department of Mechanical Engineering and his team, have proved that ethanol distilled from Ugandan cereals and tubers can power industrial machinery. The research started eight months ago.
Peter Okello-obeli, the university workshop manager, says two vehicles, Suzuki Samurai 1.5cc and Toyota Town ACE 1.8cc, which were formerly consuming petrol have responded well to the fuel, but the research is still going on. The university's four-stroke generator has also worked well with the fuel.

Original_Search_Title: Differences That Make Up Our Cultures
Headline: Uganda: Differences That Make Up Our Cultures
Body_Text:
In the second and last part of this article, Agnes Asiimwe writes that though the 24 million Ugandans live in communities having striking similarities, they also have unique ifestyles
The Bakonjo

Original_Search_Title: Banana Plantations Destroyed in Kamonyi
Headline: Rwanda: Banana Plantations Destroyed in Kamonyi
Body_Text:
Over one hundred banana plantations belonging to farmers in Kamonyi District have been destroyed in a campaign the district authorities describe as a move aimed at improving agricultural production by growing only productive crops.
In an exclusive interview with The Sunday Times, the district vice-mayor, Alice Uwera, said that the district has decided to destroy all farmers' plantations in a bid to improve their farming methods and agricultural profitability.

Original_Search_Title: Sand Wining Damaging the Livelihoods of Communities
Headline: Ghana: Sand Wining Damaging the Livelihoods of Communities
Body_Text:
Accra — The people of the Ga West District in the Greater Accra Region in the past were farmers cultivating okro, cassava, water melon and maize.  But today, the story is different, sand wining has rendered the land useless, with ponds and stagnant pools breeding mosquitoes.
The landscape is splintered with piles of sand and topsoil with uprooted tress scattered everywhere.

Original_Search_Title: Ntungamo MPs to Focus On Food Security
Headline: Uganda: Ntungamo MPs to Focus On Food Security
Body_Text:
Ntungamo — The members of Parliament in Ntungamo district have resolved to focus on the promotion of health and food security.
Kajara County MP Steven Tashobya revealed this while meeting teachers and LCs at Kajara County headquarters in Rwashamaire town on September 1. He said they had made a strength weaknesses opportunities and threat (Swot) analysis and established that malaria and food insecurity were the biggest problems faced by their voters.

Original_Search_Title: Land Reform Revisited - Lessons From Asia
Headline: Zimbabwe: Land Reform Revisited - Lessons From Asia
Body_Text:
Harare — THIS week's instalment is a departure from tradition. In the past, the focus of this column has been on recommending best practice in workforce management hence the title 'workforce solutions'. This particular instalment has nothing to do with workforce solutions but is an outline of land reform in Asia, particularly in countries such as Vietnam, Japan and South Korea.
It is this writer's view that the experiences of these countries are useful in shaping a better understanding of land reform dynamics with special reference to Zimbabwe's own Fast Track Land Reform Programme (FTLRP).

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Ready to Pay More for Regular Electricity -Survey
Headline: Nigerians Ready to Pay More for Regular Electricity -Survey
Body_Text:
"I don't mind paying more when they privatize the power holding company, if only I could have constant electricity".
That is how Chinwendu Ugwu, a roadside mobile phone operator sighted opposite the Federal Secretariat along the busy Shehu Shagari Way in Maitama, Abuja responded when confronted with the question of her readiness to pay more for improved electricity supply if the Power Holding Company of Nigeria {PHCN}is eventually privatized.

Original_Search_Title: Boosting Continent's Farm Yields
Headline: Africa: Boosting Continent's Farm Yields
Body_Text:
Emily Miyanda is luckier than most farmers in hunger-plagued Zambia. With 6 hectares of irrigated land only a dozen kilometres from the capital, Ms. Miyanda produces maize and vegetables for the big Lusaka market. She hires her neighbours to help with planting and harvesting. But even with those advantages, she is squeezed between the high costs of what she needs to buy and the low prices her produce fetches at market. "We'd like some government support in buying seeds and fertilizer," Ms. Miyanda told the BBC earlier this year. "That would make life easier."
For tens of millions of people in rural Africa, life is getting harder. Reliant on erratic rains, working exhausted soil and hobbled by decades of underinvestment and neglect by national governments and international donors, many have sunk deeper into poverty as African agriculture - the mainstay of the region's economy - continues to face neglect. A growing number of African governments and UN and non-governmental agencies argue that unless urgent efforts are made to raise crop yields, build transportation and marketing systems and adopt modern, sustainable farming methods, the continent will fail to reach its development goals and the rural majority will reap only meagre harvests.
Development blueprint
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
It is difficult to overestimate the importance of agriculture to Africa's economic prospects. More than 65 per cent of sub-Saharan Africa's estimated 750 million people are engaged in agriculture, and the sector generates more than a quarter of gross domestic product in most countries. Farm produce accounts for about 20 per cent of Africa's international trade and is a major source of raw materials for industry.
In recognition of its importance, proponents of the continental development plan, the New Partnership for Africa's Development, released NEPAD's Comprehensive Africa Agriculture DevelÂ­opÂ­ment Programme (CAADP) in 2003 (see Africa Recovery, January 2004). Its goals are ambitious - to reach rural growth rates of 6 per cent annually by 2015, integrate and invigorate regional and national agricultural markets, significantly increase agricultural exports, transform Africa into a "strategic player" in global agricultural science and technology, practice sound environmental and land management techniques, and reduce rural poverty. The plan identifies four key areas, or pillars, of an investment plan for revitalizing the rural economy, including:
expanding the acreage of irrigated African farmland and improving land management and farming techniques to preserve and improve soil quality
investing in rural infrastructure, including roads and railways, storage and processing facilities, markets, communications systems and reliable supply networks for farmers
making food production a high priority, both to combat hunger and for export, and improving emergency responses to natural disasters and conflicts
strengthening African agricultural research and development, including in advanced technologies and farming methods, and disseminating advances quickly and efficiently to farmers, suppliers and buyers.
The CAADP plan emphasizes coordinated regional action in each of the continent's diverse climate zones and relies on existing regional economic organizations and national governments for implementation. The total cost of the programme from 2002 through 2015 is estimated at $251 bn - about $18 bn a year. Although the blueprint envisages donor financing for a portion of the total, analysts note that Africa's agricultural imports currently average about $19 bn annually - suggesting that a significant portion of the costs can eventually be covered by African countries as they cut their spending on imports.
'Mined of life'
However, the challenges to success are as large as the potential consequences of failure. The UN's World Food Programme estimates that more than 30 million Africans are currently in need of international food aid. Researchers for the African Union reported in January that annual population growth has outstripped food production on the continent since 1993, resulting in a 20 per cent rise in the number of hungry people - from 176 million to 210 million. Commercial exports have fared no better, with Africa's share of world trade declining for nine of its 10 largest export crops.
One important obstacle to increased productivity has been the steady deterioration of Africa's soils, noted Mr. Amit Roy, the head of the International Fertilizer Development Centre (IFDC), a US-based institute that promotes agricultural advancement in developing countries. "Today three-quarters of Africa's farmlands - some 170 mn hectares [420 mn acres] is degraded," he told reporters in New York in late March. "As a result, grain yield has stagnated at 1 tonne per hectare, compared to the world average of about 3 tonnes." Crops consume upwards of 45 kilogrammes of nutrients and minerals from each hectare of land every season, he noted, a process known as nutrient mining. "When farmers plant the same fields season after season and cannot afford to replace the soil nutrients taken up by their crops, the soil is literally mined of life."
An estimated 8 mn tonnes of nutrients are depleted annually. Replenishing the nitrogen, potassium, phosphorus and other minerals absorbed by plants is therefore vital to keep crop yields from declining. Part of the answer lies in better farming methods, including expanding the range of crops grown, improving soil conservation practices and utilizing improved seeds and technology. But the key to launching a "revolution" in African agriculture, Mr. Roy told Africa Renewal, is much greater use of fertilizer.
In Asia and Latin America, farmers have dramatically improved harvests by improving the productivity of existing land, whereas in Africa, farmers have sought greater output by cultivating more land. "Traditionally, African farmers use the slash-and-burn method," he said. "They burn off a section of land and farm it for a season or two, then clear another plot and leave the old field fallow." But population increases and growing land shortages have forced farmers to cultivate the same fields repeatedly, stripping the land of nutrients and resulting in smaller harvests and less income. Such pressures have also led farmers to clear land poorly suited for cultivation, which contributes to soil erosion and yields only marginal increases in harvests.
An estimated 50,000 hectares of Africa's forests and 60,000 hectares of savannah are lost to such methods annually - resulting in severe environmental degradation and contributing to a 20-year decline in agricultural production per capita.
According to the CAADP, African farmers currently use far less fertilizer than their counterparts in other regions of the world. "A strong relationship exists between the level of fertilizer use and cereal yield," notes the programme.
In a study prepared for the 9-13 June Africa Fertilizer Summit, called by the African Union to focus attention on the African soils crisis, IFDC researchers Julio Henao and Carlos Baanante noted that fertilizer use in Africa is less than a tenth the world average of 100 kilogrammes. Nearly two-thirds of Africa's fertilizer is consumed in just five countries, Ethiopia, Kenya, South Africa, Zimbabwe and Nigeria, primarily in the commercial and export sectors. "Negligible fertilizer use by smallholder farmers," they asserted, "is a major factor in the region's declining farm yield per person, which has exacerbated hunger and undernutrition."
High costs, short supply
Heavy reliance on imported fertilizers, combined with high transportation costs and the absence of suppliers in the countryside, has meant that African farmers pay between two and six times the average world price for fertilizer - when they can find it at all. The IFDC study estimated that it costs more to move a kilogramme of fertilizer from an African port to a farm 100 kilometres inland than it costs to move it from a factory in the US to the port. With millions of African family farmers surviving on less than a dollar a day, imported fertilizer is simply unaffordable.
Yet evidence suggests that even modest increases in the use of fertilizer - whether nitrogen, phosphorous or potassium - can have dramatic results. In Ethiopia, one study found that just one bottle cap's worth of chemical fertilizer on each plant increased millet yields exponentially. The technique, known as "micro-dosing," is regarded as particularly appropriate for Africa's small-scale farmers, because it reduces costs and avoids damage to fragile soils from excessive chemical use.
Citing the potential environmental risks to African soils and water sources from too much chemical fertilizer being applied to farms - as sometimes happened during Asia's "green revolution" - proponents of sustainable agriculture in Africa argue that farmers should use more animal manure, compost and other organic fertilizers. If farmers better integrate stock-raising with crop cultivation, cattle and other livestock could provide them with not only more manure but also with animal traction for ploughing fields and hauling crops after they are harvested.
While organic fertilizers are important, agrees Mr. Roy, he points to a serious limitation. "The quality of animal manure is dependent on the quality of the food the animals are fed." With much soil severely depleted, he says, "the fodder contains little of the nutrients needed by crops." Organic fertilizers alone "are simply not the answer to the crisis of Africa's soil fertility. We need to increase the use of both organic and chemical fertilizers."
The CAADP makes the same point. Under the new, integrated approach to African agriculture advocated by NEPAD, "mineral fertilizers and organic matter are treated as complements rather than substitutes."
One way to make chemical fertilizers more available and affordable is to increase local production. This can reduce costs, ease the pressure on foreign currency reserves and shorten the supply chain to farmers. Although Africa consumes only about 1 per cent of global fertilizer production and currently produces even less, prospects for the commercial manufacture of fertilizers are good. Nitrogen is among the most common elements on earth, but converting it into use for plants is energy-intensive. West Africa's vast and largely untapped natural gas resources, notes Mr. Roy, therefore make the region ideally suited for the manufacture of nitrogen fertilizer. Africa also has ample deposits of phosphorus and already exports the mineral to Chinese and Indian farmers. If these minerals can be utilized in local production, Africa would need to import only potassium fertilizer.
But investment in fertilizer production will only come in response to increased demand from farmers, Mr. Roy asserts. Persuading Africa's family farmers that purchasing fertilizer is worth the money and effort will require significant improvements in rural transport networks and infrastructure, an expanded network of rural farm suppliers and markets, and greater financial returns, including protection from price fluctuations and subsidized Northern competitors.
Fertilizer no 'silver bullet'
"Fertilizer is not a silver bullet for Africa's agricultural problems," Mr. Roy admits. "The fertilizer doesn't help if it arrives too late, or the crops aren't watered or you can't sell the harvest. Farmers know this. That is why CAADP is so important. It addresses the needs of farmers comprehensively."
