In the face of erratic and unreliable rainfall in large swaths of East and Southern Africa, expanding the acreage of irrigated land is also urgent. In February, UN Food and Agriculture Organization Director-General Jacques Diouf told African agriculture ministers gathered in Bamako that just 4 per cent of sub-Saharan Africa's arable land is irrigated, compared with 38 per cent in Asia. Although much of Africa has abundant water supplies, he noted, "the region uses less than 3 per cent of its water resources, the lowest percentage of the developing world." NEPAD researchers have estimated the initial investment of irrigating 20 mn more hectares of African farmland at $37 bn, with an additional $31 bn in operating costs through 2015.
In the absence of such resources, some innovative local initiatives show promise. In Dowa, Malawi, Glyvyns Chinkhuntha has built a gravity-powered irrigation system for his 20-hectare farm using only hoes and shovels. Drawing on water from a nearby river, he has created a system of irrigation canals only as wide as a hoe blade. By blocking or unblocking individual channels with dirt, he is able to direct water where it is needed. This proves, Mr. Chinkhuntha told the US newspaper the Christian Science Monitor, that homegrown solutions to Africa's agricultural problems do exist. "Africa would have plenty to eat," he said, if farmers could gain more education and training. "All the knowledge and information is tucked up in offices," he lamented, instead of at work in the fields.
Rebuilding rural supply networks and marketing systems so farmers have the means and incentives to produce more is another major challenge. Government-run agriculture marketing boards used to perform some of these functions, providing stable prices, credit extension services, improved seeds and technology to local farmers. But as South African researchers Raj Patel and Alexa Delwiche have noted, most boards were liquidated in the 1980s and 1990s as a result of privatization policies promoted by the International Monetary Fund and World Bank. "Now, in addition to enduring direct exposure to international market fluctuations, farmers are often unsure when private buyers will appear, and are thus forced to sell cheap to the first trader," they wrote in a 2002 study for the US non-governmental organization Food First. The difficulties of transportation and shipping also mean that outÂ­lying areas often are not covered by private buyers, a problem faced by suppliers of fertilizers , seeds and other farm inputs as well.
Mr. Roy says that expanding private sector involvement in rural marketing and supply activities is a long-term solution. But he acknowledges that high poverty rates in the countryside and the need for "public goods" like roads and markets give African governments a key role in creating incentives for private investment.
In 1998, a pilot programme launched by the government and donor agencies in Malawi distributed small "starter packs" of fertilizer and improved seeds to virtually every rural household. Although the project was successful, its high cost and lack of sustainability limited its adoption on a larger scale. In Kenya, Mr. Roy says, the government chose to enable the private sector through greater investment in infrastructure, while other governments have used a combination of subsidies and private sector incentives to try to meet farmers' needs.
Finally, Mr. Roy observes, "Africa needs to do a better job in applying science and technology to agricultural problems, and getting those advances to the farmer more quickly." Here too there are encouraging signs. In 1997, scientists at the West Africa Rice Development Association developed hardy new varieties that combine the best of African and Asian types to sharply improve yields (see Africa Renewal, January 2004). NEPAD and the Nigerian-based International Institute for Tropical Agriculture are working to promote and improve another West and Central African staple, cassava, through the pan-African cassava initiative .
Expanded and more effective extension services can also bring big dividends by improving land- and water-management techniques, introducing new techniques (such as rotating crops and mixing different crops on the same farm) and bringing scientific expertise and new technologies to farmers quickly.
"The African farmer is primarily a woman farmer," Mr. Roy concludes. "And she is a good farmer who can feed her family and her continent if she is given the tools and the opportunities to do so."

Original_Search_Title: Is Cassava Africa's New Staple Food?
Headline: Africa: Is Cassava Africa's New Staple Food?
Body_Text:
It had not rained for months in Southern Africa four years ago and the fields of maize outside of Lusaka, Zambia, stood wilted and gray, a total loss. But despite one of the most severe droughts on record, Melania Chipungu's cassava fields remained vibrant green, swaying in the breeze.
"I used to grow maize, but it was difficult and I would lose my crop whenever the rain was poor. But I don't worry about the drought since I turned to cassava," Mrs. Chipungu told Africa Renewal. "Cassava is easier to grow. All I do is keep the weeds out - no fertilizers, no long hours of hard work. I have more time to meet with other women and do things that I enjoy."

Original_Search_Title: Bugesera Residents Decry Poverty
Headline: Rwanda: Bugesera Residents Decry Poverty
Body_Text:
Eastern Province — Residents in Bugesera District are decrying the severe poverty in the area, attributing it to last year's prolonged drought that hit the area. They say that their cash crops were destroyed, leading to serious food shortages, making them unable to meet essential basic needs.
In an exclusive interview with some of the residents in the area on September 1, one Jacqueline Nikunze of Nyamata Sector said this year her harvests have been very low due to low rains.

Original_Search_Title: NNPC to Import Ethanol Fuel
Headline: Nigeria: NNPC to Import Ethanol Fuel
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) yesterday said it would soon commence the importation and distribution of limited quantities of ethanol fuel into the country.
The corporation said it has awarded contract for the re-structure of two major product reception and distribution facilities at Mosimi and Atlas Cove in Lagos to facilitate the delivery and pumping of ethanol fuel to other parts of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Growing Successful in North
Headline: Uganda: Banana Growing Successful in North
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE traditional and colonial belief that bananas can not grow well in northern Uganda, has been proved wrong.
"The Langi and Acholi have missed out on banana and coffee. These crops also do well in our soils," says Grace Akullu, a prosperous banana farmer in Amac sub-county, Lira district.

Original_Search_Title: Why Rural Areas Are Not Developed - Ezea
Headline: Nigeria: Why Rural Areas Are Not Developed - Ezea
Body_Text:
Enugu — A Peoples Democratic Party gubernatorial aspirant in Enugu State, Chief Okey Ezea, has blamed the under-development of the rural areas on the failure of the local government councils to channel their resources towards the provision of infrastructures.
He also blamed the poor state of affairs in the third-tier of government to lack of monitoring, insisting that the councils needed to be monitored and made to discharge their responsibilities to the grassroots. "There has not been an integrated approach to the development of the rural areas. But this is important if we are going to develop the grassroots. The councils should be monitored and made to develop their areas. For instance, every council must rehabilitate or construct certain kilometers of roads over a period of time," Ezea said.

Original_Search_Title: New Budaka MP Calls for Unity
Headline: Uganda: New Budaka MP Calls for Unity
Body_Text:
Budaka — THE Budaka Woman Member of Parliament, Ms Pherry Kabanda Sabano, has unveiled plans to rejuvenate the district by promoting unity among the people.
The newly elected MP told Daily Monitor in an interview last Friday that the district is currently divided on political grounds, which is a hinderence to development.

Original_Search_Title: Firewood Scarcity Affects Health of Tororo Children
Headline: Uganda: Firewood Scarcity Affects Health of Tororo Children
Body_Text:
Kampala — FIFTY year-old Alice Etyang of Alupe village in Tororo, a mother of eight, considers herself lucky when she collects a small bundle of firewood in less than half a day.
She walks long distances in search for firewood, but many times returns with bundles of twigs and grass.

Original_Search_Title: Benue 2007 - Idomas Have Date With History - Hon. Idoko
Headline: Nigeria: Benue 2007 - Idomas Have Date With History - Hon. Idoko
Body_Text:
Otukpa — Hon. David Idoko is a Federal lawmaker. He represents Ado/Okpokwu/ Ogbadibo Federal Constituency in the House of Representatives. In this interview with SIMON IMOBO-TSWAM in Otukpa, the headquarters of Ogbadibo Local Government Area, Hon. Idoko opened up on his re-election bid, his support for the 2007 Idoma governorship project, his stewardship in Abuja and the poor performance of council chairmen in the state. EXCERPTS:
Sir, the general assumption is that incumbents are interested in second term. So are you too going for a second term?

Original_Search_Title: Bread. . . Any Alternative to Wheat?
Headline: Nigeria: Bread. . . Any Alternative to Wheat?
Body_Text:
Lagos — When President Olusegun Obasanjo visited the Federal Institute of Industrial Research (FIIRO), Oshodi in April 2004, he was very impressed when he was briefed about the FIIRO's research success on cassava-wheat composite bread making.
He saw the product on display, thinking that the nation will save not less than N5 billion in foreign earning if the nation can adopt a 10 per cent cassava and 90 per cent wheat composite bread, not only that, with the use of cassava flour for bread and confectioneries baking, huge post-harvest loses normally experienced by cassava farmers will be reduced and also lead to increase in cassava output due to ready market, thereby enhancing farmer's income. With this, more jobs will also be created and the nation will be better in terms of food security.

Original_Search_Title: A Dark And Stormy Night
Headline: Malawi: A Dark And Stormy Night
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Nankapakapa drifted over a greying sea, its muttering diesel pushing us into the immense expanse of Lake Malawi. Our landing was a black hump crouching in the water, almost lost in the gathering dark. A faint point of light glimmered high up on the shore, the only sign that we were in fact heading for sanctuary from the dark waters of the lake which, in the hour-long crossing from Cape Maclear, had begun to froth and heave.
A mweru had begun blowing that afternoon, even as we were travelling slowly down from Lilongwe by bus. The wind was unexpected -- this wind from the south normally starts blowing only in the spring -- and the boatman made little attempt to hide his nervousness as we embarked on the crossing. "I think I will stay here tonight," he said, as the launch thumped into a protected bay on the southern edge of the island. By now the wind was roaring, hissing through the trees, and surf was pounding the shore. We agreed. It would have been madness to go back.

Original_Search_Title: 'ANPP Can't Go It Alone' - Gov. Aleiro
Headline: Nigeria: 'ANPP Can't Go It Alone' - Gov. Aleiro
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Governor of Kebbi State ALHAJI MUHAMMAD ADAMU ALIERO was very busy, holding a round of meetings, when our reporters arrived at his Asokoro Governor's Lodge for this interview. As chairman of the ANPP's Convention Planning Committee, he was hosting several meetings all at once, in different sections of the house, and only just managed to keep this interview appointment. He spoke on many issues, including the ANPP, his administration in Kebbi State and other issues.
The ANPP national convention has been shifted by a week. As chairman of the Convention Planning Committee, are you confident that the convention will hold this time around?

Original_Search_Title: Benue And Akwaya's Notion of Development
Headline: Nigeria: Benue And Akwaya's Notion of Development
Body_Text:
Makurdi — If we go by the writings of prominent authors such as Lucyian Pye (1969) and several others we would agree that development has several angles to it such as the human aspect, infrastructure and institutional. Whenever I am writing on George Akume and the administration which he heads, I often segment his developmental strides in the following perspective: human development, infrastructure, agriculture, education, and industrialization.
By my nature I take strong reservation to joining issues with writers who out of excitement draw conclusions on issues without cross-checking their facts particularly, reacting to prejudiced write - ups that seek to promote mediocrity and ignorance about the issues at stake. my apathy to rejoinders is also borne out of the fact that it draws unnecessary attention to a myopic writer with a mind set to distort facts and mis-inform the public just because of his of her privileged position in a newspaper house, which he or she utilize to achieve personal goals.

Original_Search_Title: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Headline: Nigeria: CBN Seminar: Participants Discuss How Foreign Reserves Should Be Utilised
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The Central Bank of Nigeria organised a three day seminar on the dynamics of exchange rate management. The various resource persons argued for the effective utilisation of the current foreign reserve accumulation to launch Nigeria development agenda.
Participants at a 3-day seminar on the dynamics of managing exchange rate in Nigeria have profered workable solution on how the nation's foreign reserve should be proparly utilised to achieve maximum development for the country. Resource persons were drawn from CBN, the University community and international organisation.

Original_Search_Title: Some Labels That Define Our Cultures
Headline: Uganda: Some Labels That Define Our Cultures
Body_Text:
In the first part of a two series article, it is revealed that there are 24 million Ugandans, divided into 65 indigenous communities, according to the Constitution. Each of these groups, though have striking similarities, live a unique lifestyle. Diana Apio explores what defines these vibrant communities
The Karimojong

Original_Search_Title: FRA Helping to Market Maize and Other Crops
Headline: Zambia: FRA Helping to Market Maize and Other Crops
Body_Text:
ZAMBIA has this year yielded surplus maize production that has never been achieved since 2001. The Food Reserve Agency (FRA) is facilitating, marketing and maintaining the strategic reserves.
FRA acting food reserve and marketing manager, Richard Lisimba says the agency has managed to purchase about 172,000 tonnes of maize in less than two months, adding that they are determined to buy the remainder before the onset of the rains.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Produce to Grace Italian Expo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agric Produce to Grace Italian Expo
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE'S agricultural produce may for the first time grace exhibition shelves at the Italian International Food Exhibition in Parma in October 2007 if the current dialogue between Government, Africa Produce Project and other key stakeholders in farming is pursued to fruition.
The Minister of Agriculture, Dr Joseph Made, revealed this in Harare yesterday when he hosted the visiting Italian representatives of Africa Produce Project.

Original_Search_Title: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Headline: Zambia: Changes in Food And Nsima Culture Affect Our Lives
Body_Text:
Lusaka — SHARING and eating food with others is a sign of social communion, a "being together." The sharing from the bounty of nature's nourishment expresses unity of persons and purpose.
When people share meals, relationships deepen. We may notice that those who are mentally and socially close often would have shared some meal together.

Original_Search_Title: Delsa Calls for Repositioning of Delta Ports
Headline: Nigeria: Delsa Calls for Repositioning of Delta Ports
Body_Text:
Warri — UNDERUTILISATION of Delta ports in the face of huge potential and opportunities has been identified as the major problem facing shippers in Delta State, even as there is the need to reposition and redress the teething problems facing the ports.
The president of the Delta Shippers Association (DELSA) Mr. Edema Abrakata made these remarks weekend, in his welcome address at a seminar entitled: Promoting Non-Oil exports in Delta State "organised by the association in Warri.

Original_Search_Title: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Hugo Chavez Ends Visit
Body_Text:
Luanda — Venezuelan president Hugo Chaves Frias ended this evening his five-hour official visit to Angola, meant to reinforce the two countries' cooperation relations.
The South American nation's leader, who left the Angolan capital, Luanda at 8.20pm, headed his country delegation that held talks with the local side, meant at analysing future areas for cooperation, with highlight to oil sector.

Original_Search_Title: Nation to Strengthen Bilateral Cooperation With  Venezuela
Headline: Angola: Nation to Strengthen Bilateral Cooperation With  Venezuela
Body_Text:
Luanda — Angolan and Venezuelan Heads of State respectively José Eduardo dos Santos and Hugo Chaves, Thursday in Luanda have decided to strengthen the bilateral cooperation in various fields particularly in scientific, cultural and technological spheres.
In this ambit, according to a communiqué on the visit of the South American country's president, who arrived at 3.pm in Angola, the two leaders witnessed the signing of an oil agreement, that will boost the exploration of the two countries' potentialities.

Original_Search_Title: Group Mobilises for Egwu's Presidential Bid
Headline: Nigeria: Group Mobilises for Egwu's Presidential Bid
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Home support for Governor Sam Egwu's Presidential bid grew stronger as two groups "Divine Mandate 2007, Dr Sam Egwu for President" and "Sam Egwu Divine Mandate 2007" emerged to drum support and convince the governor to join the race.
In separate press conferences, the groups advised the Peoples Democratic Party, (PDP) to jettison its speculated intent of zoning the Presidency to any particular geo-political zone insisting that aspirants to the exalted position should be judged on their individual merits.

Original_Search_Title: Senate Rejects VAT Increase
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Rejects VAT Increase
Body_Text:
The senate yesterday rejected the 100% increase in the rate of value added tax (VAT), payable by companies and other businesses in the country, saying it will have an adverse effect on the purchasing power of citizens.
The senate, after a rigourous consideration and debate, also passed, alongside the VAT Bill, the Bill for the establishment of a Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS). The government has also abolished some levies on freight shipping, the Federal Executive Council announced in Abuja yesterday. The senators said the usual 5% should be retained as VAT and not the 10% as earlier proposed. Proponents said the bill, apart from seeking to increase the rate of the value added tax, "is aimed at using the tax to redistribute wealth as well as increase revenue yield in the country."

Original_Search_Title: Hugo Chavez in Luanda
Headline: Angola: Venezuelan President Arrives
Body_Text:
Luanda — The president of the Republic of Venezuela, Hugo Chaves Frias, arrived Thursday in Luanda at 03:07 pm (local time) with a view to strengthening bilateral cooperation with Angola.
Hugo Chavez, who has come for a few hours official visit, was welcomed at Luanda's "4 de Fevereiro" International Airport, by Foreign minister, João Bernardo de Miranda.

Original_Search_Title: Duties On Cocoa, Coal to Go
Headline: Nigeria: Duties On Cocoa, Coal to Go
Body_Text:
Abuja — FEDERAL Government yesterday abolished levies on 33 commodities to promote their export to 11 destinations in the world under a new policy code-named "Commerce 44".
The approval was granted by the Federal Executive Council (FEC) presided over by President Olusegun Obasanjo based on a memorandum presented to it by the Commerce Minister, Alhaji Aliyu Modibbo.

Original_Search_Title: Smuggling'll Worsen Joblessness
Headline: Nigeria: Smuggling'll Worsen Joblessness
Body_Text:
Abuja — MINISTER of Commerce, Dr Aliyu Moddibo Umar on Monday said that, if the activities of smugglers are not curtailed, the country will soon witness more growth in the number of the unemployed.
Moddibo, who spoke to journalists in his office in Abuja disclosed that the country would soon have a National Mudu (measurement) policy to help forestall the practice of Nigerians buying different quantities of same products at the same price.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Mills Records N86.6bn Turnover
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Mills Records N86.6bn Turnover
Body_Text:
Lagos — Flour Mills Group of companies posted an annual turnover of about N86.5 billion for the year 2005, as against N66.8 billion that was recorded in the previous year.
Its profit before taxation for the year under review stood at N6.3billion, and profit after tax and minority interest also rose from N1.5 Billion to N4.6 billion.

Original_Search_Title: Commerce 44 Initiative, Others Approved At Weekly FEC Meeting
Headline: Nigeria: Commerce 44 Initiative, Others Approved At Weekly FEC Meeting
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Executive Council, at its August 30 weekly meeting in Abuja approved the 'Commerce 44' initiative aimed at refocusing and expanding the non-oil sector of the nation's economy.
Broken down into three stages comprising committees at the federal, state and council levels, the Commerce 44 initiative involves the tax-free export of 11 categories each of solid minerals, agricultural products and groups of locally-manufactured items to 11 international markets, broken down as follows:

Original_Search_Title: FG Abolishes Export Taxes for 33 Products
Headline: Nigeria: FG Abolishes Export Taxes for 33 Products
Body_Text:
Abuja — IN its bid to encourage local manufacturers to export their products to 11 international markets across the world, the Federal Executive Council (FEC) yesterday approved the scrapping of export levies for 33 products under a new Commerce Initiative known as "Commerce 44."
By implication, the affected commodities: 11 solid minerals, 11 agricultural produce and 11 groups of locally manufactured items, are to be exempted from taxes.

Original_Search_Title: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Headline: Ghana: VP in Bilateral Business Talks With Thais
Body_Text:
Vice President Aliu Mahama on Tuesday discussed with the Thai Government Ghana's desire to boost technical cooperation in the spirit of South-South cooperation.
He used his one-and-half-hour meeting with the Deputy Prime Minister, Dr Surakiart Sathirathai in Bangkok to explain why the two developing countries should share expertise in cassava and fish production with emphasis on tuna in Ghana for the economic advantage of the two countries.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria to Earn $150m From Bio-Fuel
Headline: Nigeria to Earn $150m From Bio-Fuel
Body_Text:
Nigeria will earn the sum of $150 million annually when the bio fuel project being embarked on by the federal government comes on stream, Funso Kupolokun, the group managing director of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation [NNPC] has said.
Kupolokun who disclosed this recently at a lecture In Lagos said the project is part of the nation's alternative energy development strategy which is aimed at utilizing potentials in cassava and sugar cane to produce ethanol. He explained that under the initiative, two types of automotive fuel would be developed namely, ethanol fuel and palm oil diesel which will provide good alternative to petrol. To this end, Kupolokun said special research initiatives would be sponsored by the NNPC to boost cassava and palm oil output in the country as part of the implementation process of the project. The venture, according to him, would bring about the establishment of several ethanol production plants at an average cost of S160 million each.

Original_Search_Title: The Electoral System Must Be Reformed - Nwuruku
Headline: Nigeria: The Electoral System Must Be Reformed - Nwuruku
Body_Text:
Lagos — Recently, former Minister of State for Industry, Chief Lawrence Nwuruku expressed his intention to contest the 2007 governorship race in Ebonyi State. Nwuruku who contested the governorship of the state in 2003 under the banner of All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) is now seeking to contest on the platform of the Peoples Democratic Party ( PDP). In this interview with our Correspondent, KENNETH OFOMA, Nwuruku bares his mind on political developments in the state and why he thinks he deserves to rule. Excerpts:
What should be the major development focus of governor Egwu's successor in office?

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi to Build $80 Million Ethanol Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi to Build N10.4 Billion Ethanol Plant
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Ebonyi State Government has joined the rank of states in Nigeria seeking alternative energy sources, following the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding, (MOU), with a Chinese-supported Nigeria firm to establish a $80m(N10.4bn) Ethanol producing factory that would utilize Cassava and Sugar cane as major raw materials.
Speaking during the MOU signing ceremony which took place at the Government House, Abakaliki, Governor Sam Egwu, noted that apart from creating an unusual wealth base, the initiative would classify the state among the oil-producing states and expressed optimism that the venture would be actualized in record time.

Original_Search_Title: The Ten Percent Cassava-Flour Policy
Headline: Nigeria: The Ten Percent Cassava-Flour Policy
Body_Text:
In line with the Federal Government's policy on the production of composite flour which requires the utilization of at least 10 per cent of cassava as input, the Flour Millers Association of Nigeria (FMAN), recently established a N500 million Cassava Empowerment Fund as revolving loan to assist cassava processors in meeting their demand for cassava flour, which is currently estimated at 300,000 metric tones. Sunday Vanguard sought the opinion of stakeholders over the development. Excerpts:
ALHAJI Aliko Dangote, presi-dent, Flour Millers Asso-ciation of Nigeria (FMAN) and chairman, Dangote Group.

Original_Search_Title: Nugu Season is Back
Headline: Uganda: Nugu Season is Back
Body_Text:
Kampala — YOU could say Ragga Dee started it all with the song Nugu off his Empeta album. But in his latest album, 2005 PAM Artiste of the Year Mesach Semakula proves that the spirit of nugu songs is far from dead. His song Ndeka Nkole Bwenjagala off his 2006 album First Aid is aimed at journalists who Mesach feels have unfairly victimised him since he became a big star standing out from Eagles Production.
It would seem the season of nugu songs is upon us again because Chagga, too, has one out called Nali Kubo that accuses Jose Chameleone of having used him as a stepping stone to fame. Red Banton is also reputed to be polishing off a song that attacks Chameleone for having 'used' him to get to where he is. Red Banton then goes on to allude that Chameleone has taken to buying horses because his fellow musicians who are human beings will no longer talk to him.
